headline: Taiwanese chipmaker Nanya plans $6 billion in spending in 2027, riding AI boom
summary: Taiwanese memory chipmaker Nanya Technology said on Friday it plans capital spending of more than ‌T$200 billion ($6.2 billion) next year, roughly four times this year's figure, ‌amid soaring demand for memory chips as it rides an AI boom.  President Pei-Ing Lee told ​an online press briefing that the preliminary expenditure plan aims to help ramp up spending on a new plant, although the budget has yet to receive board approval.  Lee was speaking after Nanya reported unaudited second-quarter revenue of ‌T$82.55 billion, up 684% from ⁠a year earlier.

content.title: Taiwanese chipmaker Nanya plans $6 billion in spending in 2027, riding AI boom
content.summary: Taiwanese memory chipmaker Nanya Technology said on Friday it plans capital spending of more than ‌T$200 billion ($6.2 billion) next year, roughly four times this year's figure, ‌amid soaring demand for memory chips as it rides an AI boom.  President Pei-Ing Lee told ​an online press briefing that the preliminary expenditure plan aims to help ramp up spending on a new plant, although the budget has yet to receive board approval.  Lee was speaking after Nanya reported unaudited second-quarter revenue of ‌T$82.55 billion, up 684% from ⁠a year earlier.

headline: Chips Made In The USA Are Making A Comeback. What Investors Should Know.
summary: Semiconductor manufacturing was dominated by American companies before factories moved to countries like Taiwan and South Korea.  Now, hints of that original vigor are emerging again as a fab construction boom promises to revive what once was the global standard: computer chips made in the USA.  "Today, all of the major semiconductor manufacturers capable of building a state-of-the-art semiconductor are investing in the United States," said Joe Stockunas, president of the Americas unit of the SEMI industry trade group.

content.title: Chips Made In The USA Are Making A Comeback. What Investors Should Know.
content.summary: Semiconductor manufacturing was dominated by American companies before factories moved to countries like Taiwan and South Korea.  Now, hints of that original vigor are emerging again as a fab construction boom promises to revive what once was the global standard: computer chips made in the USA.  "Today, all of the major semiconductor manufacturers capable of building a state-of-the-art semiconductor are investing in the United States," said Joe Stockunas, president of the Americas unit of the SEMI industry trade group.

headline: South Korean billionaire's risky bet pays off, as SK Hynix debuts in New York
summary: When South Korean billionaire Chey Tae-won attends a bell-ringing ceremony on Friday for SK Hynix's $26.5 billion Nasdaq listing, it will mark the ultimate payoff of a bet many once considered risky: buying a loss-making chipmaker that has since become an AI powerhouse.  SK ‌Group's 2012 acquisition of Hynix was viewed as problematic even within the business conglomerate: Memory chips are cyclical and capital-intensive, and the company was losing money ‌while trailing Samsung Electronics in market share and technology.  But under Chey, seeking an edge over Samsung, SK Hynix has spent more than a decade betting on high-bandwidth memory (HBM) chips, at the time a niche technology.

content.title: South Korean billionaire's risky bet pays off, as SK Hynix debuts in New York
content.summary: When South Korean billionaire Chey Tae-won attends a bell-ringing ceremony on Friday for SK Hynix's $26.5 billion Nasdaq listing, it will mark the ultimate payoff of a bet many once considered risky: buying a loss-making chipmaker that has since become an AI powerhouse.  SK ‌Group's 2012 acquisition of Hynix was viewed as problematic even within the business conglomerate: Memory chips are cyclical and capital-intensive, and the company was losing money ‌while trailing Samsung Electronics in market share and technology.  But under Chey, seeking an edge over Samsung, SK Hynix has spent more than a decade betting on high-bandwidth memory (HBM) chips, at the time a niche technology.
